gate_type: no gate — authentication cookie only (wp_ajax_ prefix), no nonce, no capability check
reasoning: The only access control on ajax_dismiss_notice is the WordPress authentication layer enforced by the wp_ajax_ hook prefix (line 158), which requires a logged-in session. There is no nonce, no wp_verify_nonce(), no check_ajax_referer(), and no current_user_can() call anywhere in ajax_dismiss_notice() (lines 942-946) or its sink dismiss_notice() (lines 1023-1028). In a CSRF attack the attacker does not need to obtain the authentication cookie — the victim's browser sends it automatically when it makes the cross-site request. Therefore the authentication-only gate is fully bypassed by the CSRF vector itself: any attacker-controlled page that causes a logged-in WordPress admin to issue a GET to admin-ajax.php?action=plt_dismiss_notice&plt_notice=<N> will silently write to update_option(). No secret value exists to steal or guess. The sibling handler ajax_quick_add (lines 953-955) uses both current_user_can('edit_pages') and check_ajax_referer('plt-quick-add','plt_nonce'), confirming the developer knows the correct pattern and that its omission from ajax_dismiss_notice is the defect. Impact is limited to UI state manipulation (hiding admin notices), which keeps severity at medium rather than high.
